event: Neolithic peoples settle the coastal areas and islands of the Hong Kong region, leaving stone tools, pottery, and rock carvings.
Bronze Age communities inhabit the region, producing bronze artifacts and geometric stamped pottery during the Shang and Zhou periods.
The Qin dynasty under Emperor Qin Shi Huang conquers the Lingnan region, bringing the Hong Kong area under Chinese imperial control for the first time.
The area becomes part of the kingdom of Nanyue (Nam Viet) following the collapse of central Qin authority in the south.
The Han dynasty conquers Nanyue and incorporates the region into the empire, administering it under Panyu county in Nanhai commandery.
The Tang dynasty establishes the Tuen Mun military post to guard the maritime approaches to Guangzhou and oversee salt and pearl trade.
Imperial salt fields are operated at sites such as Kowloon and Lantau under Song dynasty administration, a major regional industry.
The fleeing Southern Song court, with the boy emperors, takes refuge in the Kowloon area while retreating from the advancing Mongols.
The Southern Song dynasty falls at the Battle of Yamen near the Pearl River estuary, ending Song resistance to the Yuan (Mongols).
The Ming dynasty builds coastal fortifications including the Tung Chung area defenses against piracy along the southern coast.
Portuguese explorer Jorge Álvares becomes the first European to reach the Pearl River estuary, landing near the Hong Kong region.
The Qing dynasty's Great Clearance forces coastal residents inland to combat Ming loyalist resistance, depopulating the area until its reversal in 1669.
Hong Kong waters and nearby coasts become a base for large pirate fleets, notably under Cheung Po Tsai, before their suppression by the Qing.
The First Opium War erupts after Qing commissioner Lin Zexu's destruction of British opium stocks, with naval clashes near Kowloon.
British forces occupy Hong Kong Island and raise the flag at Possession Point under the unratified Convention of Chuenpi.
The Treaty of Nanking ends the First Opium War and cedes Hong Kong Island to Britain in perpetuity.
Hong Kong is formally proclaimed a British Crown Colony with Sir Henry Pottinger as its first governor.
The Convention of Peking ends the Second Opium War, ceding the Kowloon Peninsula and Stonecutters Island to Britain.
The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation (HSBC) is founded in the colony, becoming a cornerstone of its financial sector.
The Peak Tram opens, providing funicular access to Victoria Peak and spurring development of elite hillside residences.
A devastating bubonic plague epidemic kills thousands in Hong Kong, prompting major public health and sanitation reforms.
The Second Convention of Peking leases the New Territories to Britain for 99 years, greatly expanding the colony's land area.
The University of Hong Kong is founded, and the colony serves as a base for Chinese revolutionaries linked to Sun Yat-sen's movement.
The Canton-Hong Kong strike and boycott, sparked by anti-imperialist sentiment, paralyzes the colony's economy for over a year.
Hong Kong's population swells with refugees fleeing the Japanese invasion of mainland China during the Second Sino-Japanese War.
Japanese forces invade and capture Hong Kong after the Battle of Hong Kong, beginning a harsh occupation that lasted until 1945.
British rule is restored after Japan's surrender, ending nearly four years of Japanese occupation and wartime hardship.
The Communist victory in the Chinese Civil War drives a massive wave of refugees and capital into Hong Kong, fueling industrialization.
The Shek Kip Mei fire leaves tens of thousands homeless, prompting the government to launch large-scale public housing programs.
Leftist riots inspired by China's Cultural Revolution erupt with bombings and strikes, marking a turning point toward social reform.
The Cross-Harbour Tunnel opens, linking Hong Kong Island and Kowloon and easing cross-harbour transport for the booming economy.
The Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) is established, dramatically reducing endemic corruption in the colony.
The Mass Transit Railway (MTR) opens its first line, transforming urban mobility in the rapidly growing territory.
Britain and China sign the Sino-British Joint Declaration, agreeing to transfer sovereignty over Hong Kong to China in 1997.
More than a million Hong Kong residents protest in solidarity with the Tiananmen Square demonstrations, deepening anxieties about 1997.
China's National People's Congress promulgates the Basic Law, the future constitutional document for the post-handover territory.
Hong Kong is handed over to China at midnight on 1 July, becoming a Special Administrative Region under the 'one country, two systems' principle.
The Asian financial crisis batters Hong Kong's economy, and the new Hong Kong International Airport opens at Chek Lap Kok.
The SARS epidemic kills hundreds, and a half-million people march against the proposed Article 23 national security legislation, forcing its shelving.
Mass protests force the government to withdraw a controversial 'national education' curriculum seen as pro-Beijing indoctrination.
The Umbrella Movement occupies central districts for 79 days demanding genuine universal suffrage for the chief executive election.
Massive pro-democracy protests erupt against an extradition bill, escalating into months of city-wide unrest and clashes.
China imposes a sweeping National Security Law on Hong Kong, drastically curbing dissent and reshaping the political landscape.
Beijing overhauls Hong Kong's electoral system to ensure only 'patriots' can govern, sharply reducing democratic representation.
